coredata.dc:title: Urea-mediated synthesized carbon quantum dots to tune the electrochemical performance of polyaniline nanorods for supercapacitor device
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Science: Advanced Materials and Devices
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: Vietnam National University, Hanoi. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar M, Sathish
K, Yamini Yasoda
Das, Puspendu
Malik, Sudip
Kothurkar, Nikhil K.
Batabyal, Sudip K.
coredata.dc:description: A polyaniline (PANi)-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite material with CQDs made from a PEG (polyethylene glycol and NaOH) source was synthesized. This unique synthesis technique was developed to overcome the setbacks of the PEG method by using UG (urea and glycine). PANi was synthesized by chemical oxidation in the presence of CQDs, which were prepared according to the two above-mentioned methods. The sample was further characterized by morphological, optical, and electrochemical experiments. FE-SEM showed ridge-like morphology and UV-Vis studies revealed the presence of PANi and CQDs. HRTEM images gave details on the uniformly distributed polyaniline, which showed lengthy rod-like rough surfaces with interconnected tiny CQDs. The electrochemical results from CV and GCD studies indicated an enhancement of the specific capacitance for sample PCQ-2 (CQDs synthesized via the UG method as a source) compared to only PANi and PCQ-1 (CQDs prepared by the PEG method). The cyclic stability established was 84.5% retention from initial capacitance values over 3000 cycles. A two-electrode prototype device was also developed with the PCQ-2 sample and it achieved a reasonable capacitance of 161.3 mF/cm2 and demonstrated good retention stability over 5000 cycles. These results may be due to a variety of factors related to compositing CQDs (with urea and glycine as a source) with PANi.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PANi
CQDs
Urea
Glycine
Supercapacitor
Cyclic stability

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CQDs from the different sources
Synthesis of PCQ (polyaniline-carbon quantum dots) composites
Fabrication of the symmetric two-electrode solid-state device
Composite electrochemical performance and comparative study
Electrochemical analysis for the sample PCQ-2
The symmetric solid-state device of the sample PCQ-2 and its electrochemical performance

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV-Vis spectra of CQDs-1 & CQDs-2 and (b) PL spectra of CQDs-1 & CQDs-2 with an excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Inset is the illuminated fluorescence image of CQDs-1 (right) and CQDs-2 (left).
FE-SEM image of (a) PCQ-2 and (b) its corresponding mapping image. (c) EDS analysis and mapping images of (d) carbon, (e) oxygen, and (f) nitrogen.
TEM images of (a) CQDs-1 (inset is the histogram of CQDs-1), (b) CQDs-2 (inset is the histogram of CQDs-2), (c) PCQ-1, and (d) PCQ-2.
UV-Vis spectra of synthesized composites: (a) PANi, (b) PCQ-1, and (c) PCQ-2.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry analysis of PANi (color-coded blue), PCQ-1 (color-coded black), PCQ-2 (color-coded red) composites at 10 mV/s (b) GCD analysis of PANi, PCQ-1, PCQ-2 at 0.4 A/g. (c) Specific capacitance bar chart with respect to the current density of 0.4 A/g of the PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 samples. (d) EIS analysis of PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 composites (inset graph is the enlarged Nyquist plot in the high-frequency region and the inset image is a corresponding equivalent circuit of the samples).
(a) CV peaks of sample PCQ-2, (b), (c), and (d) show the plot against log peak current vs log scan rate of sample PCQ-2.
(a) GCD test for sample PCQ-2 with different current densities and (b) cyclic stability of the samples PCQ-1 and PCQ-2 over 3000 cycles. The inset is the GCD test over 3000 cycles of sample PCQ-2.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry analysis of the device (inset image is the real device), (b) GCD cycles of the device at different current densities, (c) EIS test for the device with fitted data (inset image is the equivalent circuit of the device), (d) cyclic stability of the PCQ-2 device over 5000 cycles (inset is the GCD cycles of the PCQ-2 device), (e) FE-SEM image of the material before cycling, and (f) FE-SEM images of the material after cycling.
Specific capacitance measured for PANi and its composites (1, 2).

row.entry: Hydrothermal method and chemical oxidative method
Hydrothermal method and chemical oxidative method

head.ce:title: Urea-mediated synthesized carbon quantum dots to tune the electrochemical performance of polyaniline nanorods for supercapacitor device

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Excellence in Advanced Materials & Green Technologies (CoE-AMGT)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A polyaniline (PANi)-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposite material with CQDs made from a PEG (polyethylene glycol and NaOH) source was synthesized. This unique synthesis technique was developed to overcome the setbacks of the PEG method by using UG (urea and glycine). PANi was synthesized by chemical oxidation in the presence of CQDs, which were prepared according to the two above-mentioned methods. The sample was further characterized by morphological, optical, and electrochemical experiments. FE-SEM showed ridge-like morphology and UV-Vis studies revealed the presence of PANi and CQDs. HRTEM images gave details on the uniformly distributed polyaniline, which showed lengthy rod-like rough surfaces with interconnected tiny CQDs. The electrochemical results from CV and GCD studies indicated an enhancement of the specific capacitance for sample PCQ-2 (CQDs synthesized via the UG method as a source) compared to only PANi and PCQ-1 (CQDs prepared by the PEG method). The cyclic stability established was 84.5% retention from initial capacitance values over 3000 cycles. A two-electrode prototype device was also developed with the PCQ-2 sample and it achieved a reasonable capacitance of 161.3 mF/cm2 and demonstrated good retention stability over 5000 cycles. These results may be due to a variety of factors related to compositing CQDs (with urea and glycine as a source) with PANi.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors, which are similar to electrochemical capacitors, have shown great potential for efficient energy conversion/storage systems based on advantages such as high power density, fast charging and discharging rates, and long cycle stability [1–3]. Depending on the mechanism of energy storage and the electrode materials used in the system, two types of supercapacitors have been established: an electrical double layer capacitor and a pseudocapacitor. Usually, pseudocapacitors provide a much higher specific capacitance because of their reversible faradaic reactions [4–7]. This draws attention to ongoing research focused on conducting polymers and their composites to develop effective electrode materials for energy storage systems like supercapacitors and electrochemical capacitors [8–11]. In contrast to conducting polymers, PANi (polyaniline) is distinct in all aspects, like highest specific capacitance, due to its suitable multi-redox reactions, appropriate electronic properties [12] apt to protonation, good processability, low cost, and facile synthesis method [13]. These interesting properties extend the research focus in the field of a supercapacitor as an active electrode material [14]. However, poor cycling stability is a major disadvantage despite all the benefits of PANi, which is a result of volumetric swelling, shrinking during the ion doping/dedoping phase, and poor mechanical stability that hinders its electrochemical performance [15]. To overcome this hurdle, PANi is incorporated with carbonaceous materials like activated carbon, graphene, and carbon nanotubes to enhance its mechanical strength, electrochemical performance, and cyclic stability [16–20].

Numerous preceding studies have been conducted with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene, and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) on a PANi matrix to enhance its performance in the energy storage field [21–24]. Amongst various carbon materials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can reach the goal of developing the performance of PANi-based electrode materials in energy storage due to outstanding properties that emerge from quantum confinement and edge effects. Although some studies reported the use of polyaniline-carbon quantum dots (PCQ) as an electrode material for supercapacitors, detailed investigations of CQDs from different sources have not yet been explored. Zhao et al. fabricated PCQ net-like hybrid nanowires by photo-assisted CV method [25]. Wang et al. using rapid mixing polymerization have made PANi nitrogen-doped CQDs and obtained 498 F/g [26]. In this work, we have developed a facile PCQ composite from different organic sources and compared its efficiency. At first, we synthesized CQDs via the PEG method, which is a solvothermal reaction of polyethylene glycol (PEG) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) [27]. The biggest problem with this particular technique was the strong influence of sodium metal as an impurity during polymer synthesis. Indeed, removal of this metal comes at the cost of dialysis, which again is difficult due to the solubility of CQDs and sodium in water. This greatly affects the capacitance of PANi due to a disruption of the interfacial interaction of PANi and CQDs during the polymerization process. Therefore, to overcome this, we developed a unique facile technique called the UG (urea and glycine as precursors) method using both urea and glycine by a hydrothermal procedure. Earlier reports have not dealt with the combination of both urea and glycine [28–30]. Meanwhile, we use urea and glycine to our advantage to eliminate the metal impurity and thereby enhance the interfacial interaction of PANi and CQDs, which possibly leads to a significantly higher specific capacitance.

As-synthesized CQDs were used to synthesize PANi with composites adorned with CQDs that generated a ridge-like structure on an irregular PANi surface, which could maximize the material's specific capacitance. The formation of PANi composites was demonstrated by UV-visible (UV-Vis) spectroscopy, FE-SEM, and HR-TEM examinations. Electrochemical analysis was used to investigate the nature of charge storage, cyclic stability, and the synthesized composite's interfacial interactions. The two-electrode prototype device displayed real-time functions within the application.

Aniline (99%, Merck), before use, was distilled under vacuum. All other chemicals like ammonium persulfate (APS) [(NH4)2S2O8] (98%) (LobalChemie), polyethylene glycol (PEG-400) (LobalChemie Mn = 400), sodium hydroxide [NaOH] (97%) (Fisher Scientific), glycine [C6H5NO2] (98%) (Nice), urea [CH4N2O] (99%) (Nice), nafion [C7HF13O5SC2F4] (Sigma Aldrich), hydrochloric acid [HCl] (35–38%) (Nice), sulphuric acid [H2SO4] (98%) (Fisher Scientific), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) [(C2H4O)n] (98%) (Nice), isopropyl alcohol [C3H8O] (99.5%) (LobalChemie), N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone [NMP] (99.3%) (Nice), potassium bromide [KBr] (99.9%) (LobalChemie), acetone [(CH3)2CO] (99%) (Nice Chemicals, India), alumina powder [Al2O3] (99.9%) (Sigma Aldrich), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) (Sigma Aldrich), activated carbon (grade 900 IV), carbon cloth (flip India) and double distilled water have been used for preparation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs from the different sources
ce:section.ce:para:
PEG-400 (20 ml) was taken along with 1 M NaOH (10 ml) solution and sonicated for 30 min until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The homogeneous solution was then carefully transferred to a Teflon-coated stainless steel container and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. It was cooled down to room temperature and a yellowish CQDs solution was obtained. Previous reports show the room temperature and solvothermal-synthesized CQDs using PEG and NaOH as a precursor [31,32]. This was taken for further characterization and named CQDs-1.
2.
UG method - (urea and glycine)

For this particular approach, the hydrothermal method was used for synthesizing CQDs using urea and glycine. In 30 ml DI water, 1 M of urea and 1 M of glycine were added together and transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave of stainless steel. The temperature was maintained at 180 °C for 12 h and then cooled down to room temperature. A yellowish-brown colored solution of CQDs, named CQDs-2, was obtained and used for additional characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PCQ (polyaniline-carbon quantum dots) composites
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of the PCQ-1 composite, distilled aniline 1 M was used as a monomer in 1 M of 10 ml HCl aqueous solution along with the as-synthesized CQDs-1. This mixture was stirred for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous solution, and it was kept under a refrigerated bath at −5 °C with constant stirring. The oxidizing agent APS (1 M) was taken in a 50 ml burette and was added dropwise to the aniline mixture solution, which was stirred continuously for 6 h. The dark green color precipitate obtained indicated the formation of PANi. The unreacted oligomers were then washed many times with double distilled water and methanol. The final product was dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. The collected sample was designated PCQ-1 (PEG-source CQDs). The same method was used to synthesize PCQ-2 with the addition of as-synthesized CQDs-2 in place of as-synthesized CQDs-1, and this sample was named PCQ-2. For reference, pristine PANi was also synthesized by a similar process in the absence of CQDs.

Using a Shimadzu UV-Vis 1800 spectrophotometer for liquid samples, a UV-visible spectrum of the synthesized samples was obtained. Photoluminescence studies were conducted on an instrument called Shimadzu RF-6000 with a 2000-h long-life Xe lamp and an extended range PMT detector capable of scanning wavelengths of up to 900 nm. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FE-SEM) was conducted on a 30 keV Carl Zeiss Merlin compact microscope. TEM studies were conducted on a FEI TECNAI F20 at a voltage of 200 kV acceleration. The XRD was obtained using the Bruker D8 Advance instrument. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area analysis was done using the instrument Quantachrome Autosorb iQ.

An electrochemical workstation (ZIVE sp1, WonAtech Co., Ltd.) was used to perform electrochemical characterizations like cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge–discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) by using a three-electrode system. Before the measurements, a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) of 2 mm diameter with a surface area of 0.07 cm2 was polished with a 0.05 mm aluminum suspension and washed with double distilled water and finally with acetone. By mixing the active material with double distilled water, isopropyl alcohol, and Nafion, a homogeneous slurry was produced. By producing a slurry from the synthesized samples, the working electrode was prepared. Under an IR lamp, the slurry was slowly transferred onto the GCE and dried for 15 min. CV and electrochemical measurements were performed using the GCE as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference, and a platinum (Pt) wire as the negative electrode with 1 M H2SO4 as the aqueous electrolyte solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the symmetric two-electrode solid-state device
ce:section.ce:para: The symmetric supercapacitor (SSC) device was built using PCQ-2 as the positive and counter electrodes with the help of a solid-state gel electrolyte (PVA/H2SO4). The PVA/H2SO4 gel electrolyte was prepared by combining 30 ml distilled water containing 3 g of PVA at 85 °C with vigorous stirring until a homogenous solution was developed. Then, 3 g of H2SO4 was added to the mixture solution and it was allowed to cool down to form a gel at room temperature. The method of cleaning the carbon cloth substrate was performed using the same method as previously reported [30]. The active material, 50 mg of PCQ-2, was added along with 250 μl DI water, 250 μl isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and 10 μl Nafion to form a slurry to create the two-electrode symmetric device. Then, 50 μl was taken from this slurry and coated on the carbon cloth with a geometric area of approximately 1 × 1 cm2 and dried at 70 °C. The solid-state gel electrolyte was then applied to both electrodes and tightly sandwiched to avoid damage or leakage. The average mass of the active coated material on the carbon cloth substrate was 1.2 mg. Finally, the symmetric two-electrode solid-state device was successfully assembled, which can be found in more detail in the SI1 (Supporting Information).

The synthesized CQDs were characterized by different analytical techniques. In Fig. 1 (a) , UV-Vis studies of CQDs-1 (black color) and CQDs-2 (red color) in aqueous dispersions show two strong absorption peaks. Regarding the CQDs-1 sample, two absorption peaks around 253 and 312 nm were seen and assigned to the π-π∗ transition of CC within the CQDs structure, while the n-π∗ transition belongs to the oxygen-containing groups. The transition state for the CQDs-2 sample was observed with a slight shift towards the left side at 223 and 274 nm, indicating a blue-shift [33]. PL studies were conducted in aqueous media at an exciting wavelength of 340 nm to check the fluorescence properties of the synthesized CQDs (1 & 2), which is shown in Fig. 1 (b). The emission peak of the CQDs-1 sample was at 448 nm. For the CQDs-2 sample, it was at 396 nm, which indicated a blue-shift of the emission peak similar to the UV-Vis observation. This shift may be due to the multiple polyaromatic fluorophores created during the formation of CQDs through the UG process [34]. The maximum intensity of CQDs-1 was 2 lakhs, while the maximum intensity of CQDs-2 was 9 lakhs. Thus, a massive improvement in luminescence intensity can be achieved by CQDs-2 compared to CQDs-1, which resulted in a high-intensity color obtained for the CQDs-2 sample (left image in inset of Fig. 1 (b)) compared to the CQDs-1 sample (right image in inset of Fig. 1 (b)). These results show that the UG method (CQDs-2) produces good fluorescence properties.

The FE-SEM studies of PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 are shown in Fig. SI2. The FE-SEM illustration of pristine PANi (Fig. SI2 (a)) reveals a morphology similar to a cauliflower-like structure. Fig. SI2 (b) shows a similar PANi-like structure with a rough surface of sample PCQ-1. Fig. SI2 (c) shows a rough rod-like surface with a morphology of interconnected tiny pores in PCQ-2. Comparing all of these FE-SEM images, the PCQ-2 sample may be useful for improving electrochemical efficiency due to the long rod-like and porous morphology that contributes to the high interfacial interactions between polymers and CQDs. To understand the chemical composition of the composite, FE-SEM mapping and EDS analysis were done and the results are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2 (a) shows the FE-SEM image of PCQ-2, and its corresponding mapping image is shown in Fig. 2 (b), which clearly indicates a uniform distribution of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen atoms on the composite. The atomic ratios are calculated using the EDS plot of the sample PCQ-2, and the result is shown in Fig. 2 (c). The obtained atomic weight percentage of carbon was 73.65%, oxygen was 9.87%, and nitrogen was 16.48%. Separate mapping images of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen are also shown in Fig. 2(d), (e), and (f), respectively.

The high-resolution HR-TEM study was employed to test the morphology and size of carbon quantum dots on the polymer structure. The TEM image in Fig. 3 (a) suggests that the carbon dots are well distributed with an average particle size of ≈1 nm [32] in the CQDs-1 sample, and this is clearly illustrated by the histogram picture of CQDs-1 as shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (a). Fig. 3 (b) displays the TEM image of sample CQDs-2 showing that all the CQDs are evenly distributed, well scattered, and isolated from each other with an average size of 5 nm without any agglomeration. This is further demonstrated by the histogram picture of CQDs-2 shown in the inset of Fig. 3 (b). PCQ nanocomposites were taken for morphological analysis after recognizing the structure and size of only CQDs. Fig. 3 (c), the TEM image of PCQ-1, shows an agglomeration of CQDs as black spots on the PANi. Perhaps the cause for this agglomeration is Na impurities that strongly influence the interfacial interactions between PANi and CQDs, which would lead to unequal distribution on some portions of PANi with CQDs agglomeration. The TEM image of PCQ-2 suggests interwoven PANi nanorods, as shown in Fig. 3 (d), with an average aspect ratio of 300 × 50 nm. Along with the nanorods, black spots indicate an even distribution of CQDs over the PANi rods. This type of CQDs gives an active nucleation center that could lower the interfacial energy barrier, followed by a perpendicular extension of long rod-like PANi. On the other hand, in the PCQ-1 sample, the presence of sodium impurities in CQDs-1 during the polymerization process acts as an interfacial energy barrier that restricts PANi growth to long nanorods. The presence of Na + in the CQDs-1 sample is shown in the XRD of Fig. SI3, along with JCPDs card no. 02-0706. The Na + presence causes poor interfacial interaction between the domains of PANi and CQDs and also limits the mobility of electrons, which results in lower performance during electrochemical analyses. These images have shown that the PANi nanorods in the PCQ-2 sample, together with CQDs, can accommodate more channels of ion transport, more reaction active sites, as well as improve outstanding electrochemical efficiency between the electrode and the electrolyte interface [25].

UV-Vis absorption experiments were done to clarify the optical properties of the synthesized material. The spectra of PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 are shown in Fig. 4 (a)–(c), respectively. Usually, pure PANi contains two prominent wavelength peaks in the range of 310–320 nm and 410–460 nm, which are respectively associated with the polaronic transition states of π-π∗ and polaron-π∗ [9]. These two prominent peaks were also found to be maintained for the synthesized PCQ composites. The peak at 412 nm in the PANi sample was observed to have a slight red-shift in the PCQ-1 and PCQ-2 samples, as demonstrated by the composite formation [23]. These techniques confirm the successful synthesis of PANi by using two different CQDs sources. In the higher wavelength region of nearly 800 nm, the wider peak in all samples belongs to the π-polaron transition state, which shows the PANi chains in a doped state. These polaronic transitions are shown to be composed of charge carriers in the polymer structure [35,36]. Furthermore, the absorption ratio of the polaronic band to that of the π-π∗ band was analyzed, which gives the average oxidation level of the PANi nanostructure. The absorbance peak intensity ratios, A412 nm/A315 nm, A420 nm/A315 nm, A455 nm/A315 nm, and A800 nm/A315 nm were all greater than 1, which means that the PANi appears to be at a higher oxidation level [9]. The crests and troughs of the PCQ-2 sample appear to be similar to PANi, while the PCQ-1 sample exhibits very small crests and troughs indicating that the PCQ from the UG source did not affect the polyaniline structure and also maintained the material properties. However, the PANi structure is affected by the use of PEG (polyethylene glycol) and NaOH. This is due to the presence of sodium ions as an impurity, which is likely to attract chlorine atoms in the HCl dopant resulting in partially converted NaCl. Such impurities do not exist in the end product, as they are removed during washing. In any case, this causes the dopant concentration to decrease, leading to a decrease of the peak intensity of the PCQ-1 sample. In the PCQ-2 sample, a prominent enhancement in the absorbance compared to PANi and PCQ-1, near 300 nm, suggests the presence of CQDs and strong interfacial interactions between PANi and CQDs.

The surface area of the as-synthesized PCQ-2 sample was analyzed using a Quantachrome Autosorb iQ BET analyzer. Before the analysis, the sample was allowed to degas at 323 K for 6 h to remove adsorbed moisture and impurities. The N2 adsorption–desorption analysis was performed at 77 K. The multipoint BET surface area of the PCQ-2 sample was calculated from the adsorption–desorption graph shown in Fig. 5 , and it was found to be 66.4 m2/g. This available porosity in the PCQ-2 composite is very much useful for enhancing electrochemical performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Composite electrochemical performance and comparative study
ce:section.ce:para:
Electrochemical analyses were performed on pristine PANi and two distinct PANi composites. The cyclic voltammetry analysis for the samples was performed at a scan rate of 10 mV/s with a potential range from −0.4 to 0.8 V as shown in Fig. 6 (a) . An improvement in the CV curve can be observed for the PCQ-2 sample. Fig. 6 (b) (PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 samples) shows the galvanostatic charge–discharge at a current density of 0.4 A/g. Compared to the other two samples, PANi and PCQ-1, the charge and discharge time in the PCQ-2 sample increased. The calculated specific capacitance was found to be high (871 F/g) in the PCQ-2 sample compared to PCQ-1 (380 F/g) and PANi (220 F/g) samples shown in the bar chart of Fig. 6 (c). The PCQ-2 sample exhibited better enhancement in specific capacitance compared to other samples, which is due to long PANi nanorods interwoven together with CQDs that are favorable for the transportation of electrolyte ions between the electrodes and electrolyte interface. The sample's electrochemical impedance analysis is shown in Fig. 6 (d). In this figure, the Nyquist plot comprises two parts, which are the low-frequency region in the upper right and the high-frequency region in the lower-left portion of the plot. All samples show a semicircle distribution in the high frequency region and a linear slope in the low frequency region. The inset graph of Fig. 6 (d) is the enlarged Nyquist plot in the high-frequency region. An equivalent circuit for the samples is shown in the inset image in Fig. 6 (d). The fitted values for the PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 equivalent circuit elements are given in Fig. SI4. The low-charge transfer resistance can be seen in the PCQ-2 sample when comparing the other synthesized samples. In the lower frequency region, the 45° slope portion of the curve is called the Warburg resistance (frequency dependence of the process of ion diffusion/transportation). The resistance to ionic diffusion is also proportional to the slope of the impedance curves in the low-frequency region. A vertical line means that the electrode is closer to an ideal capacitor, and a broad deviation from a vertical line suggests a higher resistance towards Warburg [9]. The PCQ-2 sample has a more vertical slope, whereas the PANi and PCQ-1 samples have large deviations in the lower frequency range. These results show that the PCQ-2 sample, synthesized from CQDs using sources of urea and glycine, is a good candidate for an active material for energy storage. Thus, further electrochemical performance tests for the PCQ-2 sample were conducted. The specific capacitance (F/g) of the PANi, PCQ-1, and PCQ-2 samples, their energy density (Wh/kg), and power density (W/kg) were calculated using the Eqs. (1)–(3), respectively:
(1)
C =
i ∗ Δ t
m ∗ Δ V
(
F / g
)
(2)
E =
1 2
∗ Δ
V 2
∗
C s
(
W h
/
k g
)
(3)
P =
E
Δ t
(
W /
k g
)
where i = current (A), Δt = discharging time (s), ΔV = voltage window (V), and m = mass of the loading material (g). Table 1 provides the source of the CQDs, methods, calculated specific capacitance, energy density, power density, and cyclic stability of all the synthesized samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical analysis for the sample PCQ-2
ce:section.ce:para: PANi usually exhibits multi-redox behaviors such as leucoemeraldine (full reduced state), emeraldine (partially reduced and oxidized state), and pernigraniline (full oxidized state) during electrochemical analysis. These pairs of redox peak appearances are accounted for by the faradaic electrolyte reaction between electrodes. In Fig. 7 (a) , the A1/C1 CV peaks are a part of the transformation from leucomeraldine to emeraldine, and the minor signatures are part of the conversion from benzenoid to quinoid in the A2/C2 peaks. The A3/C3 peaks belong to the transition of emeraldine to pernigraniline [9,21] (A and C reflect anodic and cathodic peaks), which confirms the existence of PANi. The UG-sourced CQDs display EDLC behavior that can be hidden behind these peaks. The PCQ-2 sample's CV profile demonstrates strong electrochemical activity at 100 mV/s with a high current value of 1.4 mA. The peak current measurements of the anodic (A1, A2, and A3) and cathodic (C1, C2, and C3) peaks of the PCQ-2 sample are shown in Fig. 7(b), (c) and (d). All peaks display a nearly straight line with a ∼0.99 regression coefficient, suggesting good reversibility and immediate response to current oxidation/reduction due to the redox process regulated by the surface [37].

For the PCQ-2 sample, galvanostatic charge and discharge analysis was performed with varying current density (0.4–1 A/g) as shown in Fig. 8 (a) . The PCQ-2 sample's different current density GCD curve indicates the quasi-triangular curve retained in each current density, which reveals the sample's strong reversibility. Consequently, all the curves showed ohmic drops as the reason for internal resistance at the beginning phase of discharging, however, the lower charge–discharge current density of 0.4 A/g was reduced. This increase in specific capacitance and lowering of the ohmic drop at a lower current density value may be attributed to the slow electrochemical process in which the electrolyte ion's access is deep into the active material of the electrode. Considering that the ions had access to both the electrode's outer and inner surface is important. On the other hand, at higher charge–discharge current densities, a rapid electrochemical cycle occurs between the electrode and the electrolyte, which only has access to the electrode's outer surface resulting in a lower capacitance value. Fig. SI5 (a) and (b) show the cyclic voltammetry curve and GCD curve, respectively, of the PCQ-2 sample for various potential responses. The retained curve in both CV and GCD ensures the sample will operate up to 1.1 V, when the voltage varies, without any structural changes, thus indicating good bearing power. Fig. 8 (b) demonstrates the cyclic stability test for the PCQ-1 and PCQ-2 samples. The inset shown in Fig. 8 (b) is the PCQ-2 sample's GCD test of over 3000 cycles. The capacitance retention for PCQ-2 was well maintained.

The specific capacitance observed for the PCQ-2 sample was as high as 871 F/g while the other sample had lower capacitance values, i.e., PANi was 220 F/g and PCQ-1 was 380 F/g. The specific capacitances of the other PANi-based carbon materials reported earlier are shown in Table SI6 [9,25,26,30,38–42]. Despite the strong interfacial interaction between urea-glycine CQDs and the polymer, PCQ-2 has a significant enhancement in the specific capacitance relative to all other samples. Cyclic stability investigations were performed for all the synthesized samples. The PCQ-2 sample had the highest retention in specific capacitance (84.5%), while PCQ-1 had 77% over 3000 cycles. PANi had a retention of only 50% in specific capacitance over 1000 cycles due to the slow ionic rate at higher cycles, which ultimately leads to reduced retention [9]. This increase in retention for the PCQ-2 sample due to the decorated synthesized novel UG-CQDs on the PANi matrix limits the loss of adhesion of the active material or volumetric changes (because of swelling and contraction) [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The symmetric solid-state device of the sample PCQ-2 and its electrochemical performance
ce:section.ce:para: The inset of Fig. 9 (a) is an image of the actual device of the fabricated two-electrode prototype. Fig. 9 (a) shows a different scan range for the device's cyclic voltammetry. The shapes of the peaks were maintained at every scan rate, which proves the sample had better reversibility even at a higher scan rate. Fig. 9 (b) displays the GCD system profile with variable current densities. With different current densities, quasi-triangular peaks are observed without any adjustments in the GCD profile indicating improved material reversibility. Fig. SI7 (a) displays the bar chart of the specific capacitance of the areal capacitor calculated with different current densities, which only applies to the area covered. The maximum capacitance of the device was obtained as 161.3 mF/cm2 at 1 mA/cm2. We almost attained a similar result as previously reported in the literature [25] on PANi-CQDs. The Ragone plot of the respective areal device is shown in Fig. SI7 (b). The maximum energy density obtained with this areal device is 14 μWh/cm2 with a power density of 0.4 mW/cm2. Fig. SI7 (c) is a bar chart of the specific capacitance for the gravimetric device with a different current density (which only concerns the active coated mass). The maximum capacitance the device has achieved is 137 F/g at a current density of 0.8 A/g. The respective gravimetric device Ragone plot is shown in Fig. SI7 (d). The highest energy density obtained by this sample was 12.2 Wh/kg with a power density of 340 W/kg. The EIS plot of the PCQ-2 device shown in Fig. 9 (c) was plotted along with the data mounted. The raw data corresponded exactly to the fitted one, which implies supercapacitor behavior. In Fig. 9 (c), the inset image is the corresponding circuit image of the device. The first peak indicates the resistance of the device to the solution, the second is appropriate for charge transfer resistance, and the nearly straight rising line indicates the capacity behavior of the device in the low-frequency region. Adapting data values of the corresponding circuit image of the device is defined in Fig. SI8. The cyclic stability of the device revealed in Fig. 9 (d) includes the use of long-term output without any breakdown or discontinuity in specific capacitance (i.e. 85 % retention) over 5000 cycles at a current density of 3 mA/cm2. FE-SEM images were taken before and after the cycling process to understand the structural changes of the PCQ-2 composite. The rod-like interconnected surface can be seen in the FE-SEM image of the electrode, before cycling process, is shown in Fig. 9 (e). The morphology of PCQ-2 maintained its rod-like shapes after the cycling process (over 5000 cycles) as shown in Fig. 9 (f). Because of interactions with the electrolyte used in the device, there are some agglomerated rods visible in Fig. 9 (f). The PANi material decorated with novel synthesized urea glycine CQDs can be considered as an attractive substrate for active supercapacitor electrodes.

Prepared PCQ nanocomposites (1 & 2) were assessed for their application as supercapacitor materials. PANi provides a pseudo-capacitance to the composite and the carbon quantum dots generated using urea and glycine as the source provides a high surface area, enhanced transportation of electrons, and potentially increased durability. The PCQ-2 nanocomposite was found to synergistically outperform the pristine PANi and PCQ-1 nanocomposite with a reasonable capacitance of 871 F/g. This sample also showed a moderate retention lifetime after 3000 cycles of 84.5%. The two-electrode prototype device was also made with the PCQ-2 sample and achieved a specific capacitance of 161.3 mF/cm2, which also showed good retention stability over 5000 cycles. This device can produce a 14 μWh/cm2 energy density at a power density of 0.4 mW/cm2. This may be caused by a combination of factors stemming from compositing CQDs (urea and glycine) with PANi. Thus, the PCQ composite derived from the combination of urea and glycine sources can be considered as a promising supercapacitor electrode material due to their peculiar morphology and favorable interfacial interactions.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: SM is indebted to CSIR, New Delhi, India (project no. 01(2875)/17/EMR-II) for financial support. The first author M. Sathish Kumar is indebted to CSIR New Delhi, India (project no. 09/1034(0005)/2K19-EMR-I). P. Das is thankful to DST INSPIRE, India for the fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsamd.2021.11.005.
